verses.text: Samuel avait indiqué que ces trois éléments, signes distincts de la royauté, sont les premiers que le roi prendrait au peuple ( **1S 8.11** ). Un autre fils de David, Adonia, a rassemblé les mêmes biens lorsqu'il convoitait le trône ( **1R 1.5** ).

Absalom se révolte contre David onze ans après qu'Amnon ait violé Tamar (cf. **13.23** , **38 ** ; **14.28 ** ; **15.7** ), approximativement dans la vingt-cinquième année du règne de David, qui a duré trente-trois ans.\n\n

Absalom se révolte contre David onze ans après qu'Amnon ait violé Tamar (cf. **13.23** , **38 ** ; **14.28 ** ; **15.7** ), approximativement dans la vingt-cinquième année du règne de David, qui a duré trente-trois ans.\n\n

Absalom se révolte contre David onze ans après qu'Amnon ait violé Tamar (cf. **13.23** , **38 ** ; **14.28 ** ; **15.7** ), approximativement dans la vingt-cinquième année du règne de David, qui a duré trente-trois ans.\n\n
Se prosterner devant Absalom, c'était en fait l'honorer en tant que roi (voir **14.33** ).
Absalom, par son accessibilité et le prétendu intérêt qu'il porte au peuple, gagne sa confiance.

Dans le texte hébreu, on lit quarante ans, ce qui daterait le soulèvement d'Absalom dans la dernière année du règne de David et créerait de nombreuses difficultés chronologiques avec les événements qui se sont produits par la suite (voir aussi note d'étude sur 15.1-19 ). La lecture grecque et syriaque est beaucoup plus probable. • Contrairement à l'offrande volontaire de dévotion et de gratitude qu'un adorateur promettait à Dieu, ce type d'offrande était destiné à remplir une promesse faite à Dieu (voir **Gn 28.20-22 ** ; **Jg 11.30-31 ** ; **1S 1.11** ). Absalom mentait probablement (cf. **1S 20.28-29** ).

Après avoir tué Amnon, Absalom s'était réfugié dans la patrie de sa mère (voir aussi les notes d'étude sur 13.34-39 , 37 ). • L'endroit idéal pour qu'Absalom transforme sa popularité en une véritable révolte contre David est Hébron, la première capitale de David.

15:9 And the king said unto him, go in peace,.... He gave him leave to go, and wished happiness and prosperity might attend him: so he arose and went to Hebron; with a company of men, whose number is after mentioned.

15:10 HE FORMS A CONSPIRACY. (2 Sam 15:10-12) Absalom sent spies throughout all the tribes of Israel—These emissaries were to sound the inclination of the people, to further the interests of Absalom, and exhort all the adherents of his party to be in readiness to join his standard as soon as they should hear that he had been proclaimed king. As the summons was to be made by the sound of trumpets, it is probable that care had been taken to have trumpeters stationed on the heights, and at convenient stations—a mode of announcement that would soon spread the news over all the country of his inauguration to the throne.

15:11 with Absalom went two hundred men . . . that were called—From their quality, reputation, and high standing, such as would create the impression that the king patronized the movement and, being aged and infirm, was willing to adopt his oldest and noblest son to divide with him the cares and honors of government.

Ahitofel était le grand-père de Batchéba (cf. **11.3 ** ; **23.34** ) ; des années plus tard, il était peut-être encore en colère après ce que David avait fait à sa petite-fille. • Guilo était situé dans la région d'Hébron ( **Jos 15.51** , **54** ).

15:13 The hearts of the men of Israel are after Absalom - It is very difficult to account for this general defection of the people. Several reasons are given: 1. David was old or afflicted, and could not well attend to the administration of justice in the land. 2. It does appear that the king did not attend to the affairs of state, and that there were no properly appointed judges in the land; see 2 Sam 15:3. 3. Joab's power was overgrown;

15:14 DAVID FLEES FROM JERUSALEM. (2Sa. 15:13-37) David said . . . Arise, and let us flee—David, anxious for the preservation of the city which he had beautified, and hopeful of a greater support throughout the country, wisely resolved on leaving Jerusalem.
Il est possible que David ait laissé derrière lui dix de ses concubines parce qu'il pensait que son exil serait de courte durée. Voir **16.21-22** .
Il est possible que David ait laissé derrière lui dix de ses concubines parce qu'il pensait que son exil serait de courte durée. Voir **16.21-22** .

15:17 And the king went forth,.... From Jerusalem; which is repeated, that it might be observed in what a hurry and fright he was: and all the people after him; his family, court, and servants, and as many of the people of Jerusalem as chose to go with him: and tarried at a place that was afar off; when they had got at some distance from the city, they stopped and stayed a while; it could not be a great way from it, for they had not as yet passed over the brook Kidron, 2 Sam 15:23.
Cette alliance remonte aux relations de David avec les philistins à l'époque de Saül ( **1S 21.10-15 ** ; **27.1-12 ** ; **29.1-11** ). • Au sujet de la garde, voir note d'étude sur 2S 8.18 .

Ittaï, philistin de Gath, était associé à Guibéa en Benjamin ( **23.29** ; **1Ch 11.31** ) ; les philistins avaient établi une garnison à Guibéa au début du règne de Saül ( **1S 10.5 ** ; **13.3** ). • David laisse place au roi Absalom, qui a réussi à gagner la confiance d'Israël.

15:20 Whereas thou camest but yesterday,.... From Gath, or from an expedition he and his men had been on: should I this day make thee, go up and down with us? wander up and down from place to place with David, when he was but just come off a journey, weary and fatigued: seeing I go whither I may; where it will be most safe for me, I know not where; may be obliged to flee here and there, which would be very inconvenient to Ittai in his circumstances: return thou, and take back thy brethren;

15:21 And Ittai answered the king, and said,.... With an oath, as follows: as the Lord liveth, and as my lord the king liveth; which he took to confirm what he after says, and to put an end to the debate between them: surely, in what place my lord the king shall be, whether in death or life, even there also will thy servant be; signifying that he would attend him wherever he went, hazard his life in his cause, and live and die with him.

15:22 And David said unto Ittai, go, and pass over,.... It being his resolution to abide with him, he urged him no more to depart, but bid him pass over the brook Kidron before him: and Ittai the Gittite passed over, and all his men; the six hundred Gittites that were under his command: and all the little ones that were with him; that belonged to him and his men, and no doubt their wives also.
La vallée du Cédron, à l'est de Jérusalem, séparait la cité de David du mont des Oliviers.
Sadoc était l'un des deux prêtres au service de David ( **8.17** ). Plus tard, il a oint Salomon ( **1R 1.39** ) et est devenu grand prêtre après la destitution d'Abiatar ( **1R 2.26-27** ).

Les personnes qui fuyaient les invasions emportaient généralement leurs idoles pour éviter qu'elles ne tombent entre les mains de l'ennemi. La capture d'un dieu pouvait être interprétée par les rebelles comme un signe que les dieux avaient abandonné la dynastie qui régnait jusque-là. Cependant, l'Arche n'était pas une simple idole ou un talisman (cf. **1S 4.3-11 ** ; **5.6–6.9** ). Elle était l'objet central du sanctuaire et représentait la présence de l'Éternel, le Dieu d'Israël (voir **Ex 40.34-35** ). David a volontairement laissé l'Arche tomber entre les mains d'Absalom, confiant dans la grâce et les promesses de Dieu ( **2S 15.26 ** ; voir **7.8-16** ) que l'Arche soit avec lui ou non.

15:26 But if he thus say, I have no delight in thee,.... As a king, or in his temporal prosperity, though he might and had delight in him as a chosen vessel of salvation, as a saint and child of God, and in his spiritual and everlasting welfare: behold, here am I; his humble servant, ready to be, and do and suffer whatever is his pleasure: let him do to me as seemeth good unto him; strip me of all the ensigns of royalty, dispossess me of my crown and kingdom, and dispose of me as seems good in his sight;
Ahimaas et Yonatan ont soutenu David lors du coup d'État d'Absalom, en lui servant de messagers secrets (voir **15.36 ** ; **17.17-22** ).

15:28 See, I will tarry in the plain of the wilderness,.... Towards the way of which David and the people went when they came over Kidron, 2 Sam 15:23, until there come word from you to certify me; of the truth of the conspiracy, of the number of the conspirators, and who they are, what progress they have made, whether come to Jerusalem, and how they behave there, if come; or of anything relative hereunto he could get intelligence of.

15:29 Zadok therefore and Abiathar carried the ark of God again to Jerusalem,.... That is, ordered it to be carried, and took care that it was carried, by the Kohathite Levites, and they themselves attended it: and they tarried there; at Jerusalem; though their two sons that went with them entered not into the city, but stayed at a place called Enrogel, at some little distance from it, 2 Sam 17:17.

15:30 David went up by the ascent of mount Olivet—The same pathway over that mount has been followed ever since that memorable day. had his head covered—with a mourning wrapper. The humility and resignation of David marked strongly his sanctified spirit, induced by contrition for his transgressions. He had fallen, but it was the fall of the upright; and he rose again, submitting himself meekly in the meantime to the will of God [CHALMERS].
David a également envoyé un agent double, Houchaï, pour faire échouer les conseils d'Ahitofel ( **15.32-37 ** ; **17.1-14** ).

À cette époque de l'histoire d'Israël, les gens adoraient Dieu sur de multiples autels à travers le pays. Le Tabernacle, tel qu'il existait à l'époque de Josué et de Samuel, avait disparu et le Temple n'était pas encore construit. • Un Arkite était probablement un non-Israélite de la région située au sud de Béthel ( **Jos 16.2** ).

15:33 Then thou shalt be a burden unto me - It appears that Hushai was not a warrior, but was a wise, prudent, and discreet man, who could well serve David by gaining him intelligence of Absalom's conspiracy; and he directs him to form a strict confederacy with the priests Zadok and Abiathar, and to make use of their sons as couriers between Jerusalem and David's place of retreat.
Tout en étant persuadé que la providence de Dieu était à l'œuvre dans les coulisses, David a également pris des mesures stratégiques pour faire avancer sa propre cause.

15:35 And hast thou not there with thee Zadok and Abiathar the priests?.... To assist in forming schemes directly opposite to Ahithophel's, or to whom he could communicate the secrets of Absalom's court: therefore it shall be that what thing soever thou shalt hear out of the king's house; Absalom's, who had now, at possession of the house and palace of David: thou shalt tell it to Zadok and Abiathar the priests; to whom he might have recourse without suspicion, pretending he had business with them as priests, on religious accounts, to offer sacrifices for him, &c.

15:36 Behold, they have there with them two sons, Ahimaaz, Zadok's son, and Jonathan, Abiathar's son,.... As in 2 Sam 15:27; not that they were in the city with them, but they were near it, 2 Sam 17:17; with whom they had a communication: and by whom ye shall send unto me everything that ye can hear; that is, by the sons of the priests; he telling the priests how things were at court, and they sending their sons with messages to David;

15:37 Absalom came into Jerusalem - It is very probable that he and his partisans were not far from the city when David left it, and this was one reason which caused him to hurry his departure. Reader, behold in the case of David a sad vicissitude of human affairs, and a fearful proof of their instability. Behold a king, the greatest that ever lived, a profound politician, an able general, a brave soldier, a poet of the most sublime genius and character, a prophet of the Most High God, and the deliverer of his country, driven from his dominions by his own son, abandoned by his fickle people, and for a time even by his God!
